title: Lava Tube Mapping
desc: A quake opened new formations. Someone has to chart them.

steps.text:
Last night's quake wasn't big — a 3 on whatever scale the UnderWorld uses — but it cracked open a section of lava tubes that nobody knew existed. Steam vents from the fresh openings, and the air smells of hot metal.

A surveyor is setting up mapping equipment at the entrance to the largest tube. "New tubes mean new routes, new resources, or new problems. My job is to figure out which."

She hands you a spool of mapping wire — thin metal cord you trail behind as you walk, creating a physical record of the path. "You go in. I'll triangulate from here. Don't lose the wire. If it breaks, you don't come back the same way — these tubes branch."

options.label: Enter the tube — trail the wire
Check the tube entrance structurally first

steps.text:
The tube is smooth inside — lava-polished, glassy. Your footsteps echo strangely, the sound stretching ahead of you like a scout. The wire unspools behind you, a lifeline of thin metal.

The tube branches at fifty feet. Left slopes down — warmer, steam rising. Right curves level and dry. You take the right branch first, mapping as you go.

The dry branch opens into a chamber. Small, circular, and covered in crystals that grew in the cooling lava — natural formations, glittering in your lamp-light like frozen sparks.

"Chamber at seventy-two feet," you call back. The surveyor's voice returns, distorted by distance: "Crystal deposit?"
"Significant."
"Mark it and come back. The miners will want to see this."

You tie a flag to the wire and retrace your steps. The left branch can wait for the next survey. One discovery per day is plenty.

Smart. The entrance is fresh — cracked open by the quake, edges sharp. You tap the surrounding stone with a hammer. Solid. No secondary fractures, no loose material overhead. The tube can hold.

But you notice something at the entrance lip: old marks. Chisel marks. Deliberate cuts in the stone, half-hidden under the fresh break.

Someone sealed this tube. Intentionally. A long time ago.

The surveyor examines the marks. "These are old. Really old. Pre-settlement." She runs her fingers along the chisel lines. "Someone filled this entrance with stone and mortar. The quake just unsealed it."

"Why seal a lava tube?"

She doesn't answer. She maps the entrance, logs the chisel marks, and requests a deeper survey team with security escort.

"Because maybe it wasn't about keeping things out," she says finally. "Maybe it was about keeping something in."

The tube exhales warm air. It doesn't smell like lava. It smells like deep water.
